findings.suggestion: Skip dangerous keys before touching target: at the top of the loop add `if (key === '__proto__' || key === 'constructor' || key === 'prototype') continue;`. Additionally, guard the recursion/assignment with `Object.hasOwn(source, key)` and prefer null-prototype accumulators (Object.create(null)). Add a regression test: `merge({}, JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"polluted":true}}')); expect(({}).polluted).toBeUndefined()` plus a constructor.prototype variant.
findings.summary: merge() at source.ts:9-21 iterates Object.keys(source) (line 11) with no key blocklist. For target[key]/source[key] where key='__proto__', tv resolves to Object.prototype (an object) and sv is an object, so lines 14-15 recurse into merge(Object.prototype, sv), and line 17 (target[key]=sv) then writes attacker-controlled keys onto the shared prototype. Traced: merge({}, JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"admin":true}}')) sets Object.prototype.admin=true, polluting every object app-wide. The function is exported (public API) so any untrusted source (JSON body, query params) is exploitable.
findings.title: Prototype pollution: merge() copies __proto__/constructor/prototype keys onto Object.prototype

findings.suggestion: In the loop (line 11) skip dangerous keys: `if (key==='__proto__'||key==='constructor'||key==='prototype') continue;`. Also only recurse/read own properties (Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(source,key)) and consider building results on Object.create(null) or using a Map so inherited-key access can't reach Object.prototype.
findings.summary: merge() (source.ts:9-21) iterates Object.keys(source) without excluding __proto__/constructor/prototype. For JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"admin":true}}'), Object.keys returns ['__proto__'] (own enumerable prop). Line 12 tv=target['__proto__'] returns Object.prototype (object), line 13 sv=source['__proto__'] returns the own {admin:true} (object), so line 15 recurses merge(Object.prototype,{admin:true}) and line 17 sets Object.prototype.admin=true — every object in the process then reports .admin===true, silently flipping auth/flag flows into wrong states. Currently 0 callers, so no live path yet, but must be fixed before any caller is added.
findings.title: Prototype pollution in exported merge() escalates privileges app-wide

findings.suggestion: Skip dangerous keys before assignment/recursion: `if (key === '__proto__' || key === 'constructor' || key === 'prototype') continue;`. Additionally guard with Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(source,key) and/or build results with Object.create(null) / use a Map, and only recurse into own plain objects. Add a regression test asserting ({}).admin is undefined after merging a __proto__ payload.
findings.summary: merge() (exported, lines 9–20) iterates Object.keys(source) (line 11) and, for a JSON-parsed key "__proto__", tv=target["__proto__"] (line 12) resolves to Object.prototype and sv is the attacker payload; both pass isObject, so line 15 recurses merge(Object.prototype, {...}) and line 17 writes attacker-controlled keys onto Object.prototype. Traced end-to-end: JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"admin":true}}') makes __proto__ an own enumerable key, so ({}).admin becomes true app-wide. This is a real, exploitable vulnerability, not just documentation.
findings.title: Prototype pollution in exported recursive merge via __proto__

findings.suggestion: Decide intent: if this is production code, apply the key-blocklist fix above and delete the BUG comment; if it is a benchmark fixture, mark it as such so no agent 'fixes' it silently. Either way attach fresh verification — add and run a prototype-pollution test (the smallest relevant command for this diff) before marking resolved, since the pre-review gate 'rerun_relevant_verification' is blocked with no fresh command/test evidence.
findings.summary: Lines 6-8 contain a `// BUG:` comment that fully diagnoses the prototype pollution, yet the code below (lines 9-21) implements exactly that vulnerable behavior with no mitigation. The repo has no test file and no caller for the exported merge (blast-radius: 0 callers, newly added), so nothing verifies the security contract. This is either an incomplete handoff (the fix was the deliverable and was never applied) or an intentional fixture — the next agent must not treat the annotated-but-unfixed state as done.
findings.title: Documented-broken code shipped with no fix and no verification test

findings.suggestion: Correct the comment to state that __proto__ is the live vector here (constructor/prototype are blocked incidentally by isObject rejecting functions), while still recommending all three be explicitly denied for defense-in-depth against future refactors of isObject.
findings.summary: The comment (lines 6–7) claims merge fails to block `constructor` and `prototype`, implying those are live pollution vectors. But isObject (line 3) returns false for functions (typeof Object === 'function'), so for key 'constructor' tv=target['constructor'] fails the isObject(tv) check on line 14 and falls to line 17, setting an own property only — it does NOT reach Object.prototype. The only live vector in this implementation is __proto__. A fixer trusting the comment could mis-scope the guard or waste effort on a non-path.
findings.title: Comment overstates the constructor/prototype pollution path (assumption integrity)

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

New file source.ts introduces an exported recursive merge() with a critical, confirmed prototype-pollution vulnerability via the __proto__ key (JSON-parsed payload → Object.prototype mutation, verified by static trace of lines 11–17). The accompanying comment accurately flags the __proto__ vector but overstates the constructor/prototype paths, which isObject's function check actually blocks in this implementation — a minor assumption-integrity issue that could mis-steer a fixer. Fix must deny __proto__/constructor/prototype keys and add a regression test.

Single new file adding an exported recursive merge(). It contains a real, reachable prototype-pollution vulnerability (source.ts:11-17): __proto__/constructor/prototype are not filtered, so a JSON payload like {"__proto__":{"admin":true}} mutates Object.prototype and grants attacker-controlled properties to every object process-wide — an app-wide privilege-escalation / confusing-state defect. Confirmed by static trace of standard JS semantics (JSON.parse creates an own enumerable __proto__ key that Object.keys enumerates; target['__proto__'] resolves to Object.prototype). CAVEAT: blast-radius reports 0 callers, so there is no active exploit path today — severity reflects impact-if-used and the need to fix before wiring it in. The rerun_relevant_verification gate remains BLOCKED: I attempted a node repro but the sandbox denied node execution in this non-interactive session, so no fresh runtime artifact is attached; the finding is static-analysis-based (high confidence given the textbook mechanism).

The diff adds a single exported merge() with a critical, self-documented prototype pollution vulnerability: user-controlled __proto__/constructor keys are walked without a blocklist (source.ts:11), and the recursion at lines 14-15 plus assignment at line 17 write onto Object.prototype. Exploit path verified by trace. No tests, no callers, and the vulnerability's own BUG comment is left unaddressed — a documented-broken handoff. The bounded fix is a three-key guard at the top of the loop plus a null-prototype accumulator and a regression test.
